* Figures

This file includes notes on generating the figures used in the report, and
additional testing information, that lead to our selections.

** Inspection

We have a lot of data that needs investigation.  The very first thing we can do
is just look at the days and DAU's that have the largest variations, in
ETo. We can look at Symsoft over estimations (+ diff) and under estimations.

On the over estimation side, you see that these are

#+begin_src sql
  select date,doy,dau_code,ETo_average,eto_d_diff_average
  from stats
  where date not in ('2024-05-08','2024-05-23','2024-03-14')
  order by eto_d_diff_average desc
  limit 25
#+end_src

When you look the highest diffs (order desc), you can see this is mostly because
of of a few days, which you can remove a few of these days ~where date not in
('2024-05-08','2024-05-23','2024-03-14')~

a particular dau_code.  If you look, dau_code 347, Yuma, is the south-eastern
most DAU.

** Plot One DAU region for the entire year

However, if you end up looking at the data for this location, it actually looks
fine.  If anything, we see there is a sort of a bias where the symsoft is
higher.

#+begin_src sql
      select date,doy,eto_average,eto_d_diff_average,tx_average,tx_d_diff_average
      from stats
  where dau_code='347'
    order by doy
#+end_src

#+begin_src gnuplot :var data=dau347 :exports code :file images/dau347.png
  set title "DAU"

  set xlabel "DOY"
  set xrange [0:182]
  set xtics 0,10,185


  set ylabel "ETo [mm]"
  set yrange [-2:12]
  set ytics -2,2,12


  plot data u 2:3 w p lw 2 title 'ETo', \
       data u 2:4 w lp lw 1 title 'ETo diff'
#+end_src

#+RESULTS:
[[file:images/dau347.png]]

Could that be Tx?  Actually, when you look that looks okay as well.

#+begin_src gnuplot :var data=dau347 :exports code :file images/dau347_Tx.png
  set title "DAU"

  set xlabel "DOY"
  set xrange [0:182]
  set xtics 0,10,185


  set ylabel "Tx"
  set yrange [-5:45]
  set ytics -5,5,45

    set y2label "Tx diff"
  set y2range [-5:10]
  set y2tics -5,1,0


  plot data u 2:5 axis x1y1 w p lw 2 title 'Tx', \
       data u 2:6 axis x1y2 w lp lw 1 title 'Tx diff'
#+end_src

#+RESULTS:
[[file:images/dau347_Tx.png]]

** Look at bad days

What about other days where we have a lot of regions that are different

#+begin_src sql
  with d as (
    select date,ETo_average,eto_d_diff_average,
  	 (eto_d_diff_average*10)::integer as d
      from stats
  ), dt as (
    select date,d,count(*)
      from d
     group by d,date
     order by d,date
  )
  select date,count(*)
    from dt
   where d>10 or d<-10
   group by date
  having count(*) > 10;
#+end_src

#+RESULTS:
|       date | count |
|------------+-------|
| 2024-03-14 |    11 |
| 2024-05-23 |    22 |
| 2024-05-08 |    15 |


#+name: oneday
#+begin_src sql
      select dau_code,eto_average,eto_average+eto_d_diff_average as eto_dish,Rs_average,Rs_average+Rs_d_diff_average as Rs_dish
      from stats
  where date='2024-05-23'
    order by dau_code
#+end_src

#+begin_src gnuplot :var data=oneday :exports code :file images/day_523.png
  set title "ETo for 2024-05-23"

  set xlabel "DAU Code"
  set xrange [1:405]
  set xtics 0,25,400

  set ylabel "ETo"
  set yrange [0:10]
  set ytics 0,1,10

  set y2label
  set y2range [0:0]
  set y2tics

  plot data u 1:2 axis x1y1 w p lw 2 title 'ETo Symsoft', \
       data u 1:3 axis x1y1 w p lw 1 title 'ETo Dish'
#+end_src

#+RESULTS:
[[file:images/day_523.png]]

If you look at this, you can see that there is a big bias for this particular
day, and it's all due to Rs.

#+begin_src gnuplot :var data=oneday :exports code :file images/Rs_523.png
  set title "Rs for 2024-05-23"

  set xlabel "DAU Code"
  set xrange [1:405]
  set xtics 0,25,400

  set ylabel "Rs"
  set yrange [0:50]
  set ytics 0,5,50

  plot data u 1:4 axis x1y1 w p lw 2 title 'Rs Symsoft', \
       data u 1:5 axis x1y1 w p lw 1 title 'Rs Dish'
#+end_src
